seed_prompt: A playful hand-drawn technical comic, 4 panels, teaching one idea: after restart, recovery starts from the last checkpoint, walks forward in the WAL, ignores a broken tail, and applies the saved changes to disk. Style: simple educational cartoon, expressive characters, wobbly hand-drawn lines, thick outlines, white paper background, bright but limited colors, fun and readable, like a Julia Evans style tech cartoon, not a corporate diagram. Cast: professor inu shiba dog with round glasses as the teacher, a long WAL scroll/notebook, and the disk-tree character. Panel 1: professor shiba stands next to WAL and disk tree and says 'Start from the last checkpoint'. The old part of the WAL is faded out before a checkpoint marker. Panel 2: professor shiba points at the WAL after the checkpoint marker and says 'Read from here'. Show a few WAL notes after the checkpoint marker. Panel 3: professor shiba follows the WAL notes forward, then reaches a torn or broken tail at the end and says 'This part is broken. Ignore it.' Make the torn tail a little funny. Panel 4: professor shiba hands a note labeled 'list of LogOps' from the WAL to the smiling disk tree and says 'Apply these, then checkpoint.' Use very few words. Let the scene teach the concept. No transaction jargon, no node labels, no low-level implementation text.
draft_prompt: A four-panel hand-drawn educational comic on textured white paper, in the style of Julia Evans. The style uses wobbly thick black outlines, bright limited crayon colors (blues, yellows, greens, reds), and expressive, cute characters. Panel 1 (Top Left): Professor Inu Shiba, a cute shiba dog wearing round glasses and a tweed jacket, stands next to a long, unrolling scroll character labeled 'WAL' (Write-Ahead Log) and a smiling tree character with a hard disk drive for a trunk, labeled 'DISK-TREE'. The WAL scroll has a distinct red stamp marker labeled 'CHECKPOINT'. The part of the scroll *before* the checkpoint is greyed out and faded. Professor Shiba points to the marker, saying in a speech bubble: 'Start from the last checkpoint!' Panel 2 (Top Right): A close-up on the WAL scroll *after* the 'CHECKPOINT' marker. Professor Shiba points excitedly at a series of distinct, readable notes drawn on the scroll (e.g., small drawings of a nut, a water drop, a leaf with checkmarks). Professor Shiba says: 'Read forward from here.' Panel 3 (Bottom Left): Professor Shiba follows the scroll to its very end, which is ragged, torn, and emitting cartoon smoke and squiggles. The notes on this torn section are illegible scribbles. Professor Shiba makes a dismissive gesture at the torn end, saying: 'Broken tail? Ignore it!' Panel 4 (Bottom Right): Professor Shiba holds a tidy stack of the valid notes collected from Panel 2, labeled 'list of LogOps' with a bow on it, handing them to the happy Disk-Tree character. The Disk-Tree holds a large rubber stamp ready to mark its trunk. Professor Shiba says: 'Apply these. Then make a new checkpoint!' The Disk-Tree gives a thumbs-up.

The style uses wobbly thick black outlines, bright limited crayon colors (blues, yellows, greens, reds), and expressive, cute characters. draft_payload.story_purpose: To visually explain the database crash recovery process: resuming from the last known good state (checkpoint) in the log, replaying valid subsequent entries, discarding incomplete corrupted data at the end, and committing the good data to permanent storage.
key_elements: 4-panel comic layout on white paper texture
Professor Inu Shiba character (teacher with glasses)
Disk-Tree character (tree with hard drive trunk)
Visual distinction between 'faded/old' log entries, 'valid/new' log entries, and 'broken/corrupted' tail entries
labels_icons: Label on note stack: 'list of LogOps'
Visual icons on WAL notes: nuts, water drops, leaves (representing abstract data changes)
draft_payload.style_notes: Julia Evans tech comic style: hand-drawn, wobbly lines, thick outlines, friendly and expressive anthropomorphic objects, bright but limited crayon color palette, textured paper background. Typography should be hand-lettered and friendly.

final_prompt: A four-panel hand-drawn educational comic on textured white paper, in the style of Julia Evans. The style uses wobbly thick black outlines, bright limited crayon colors (blues, yellows, greens, reds), and expressive, cute characters. Panel 1 (Top Left): Professor Inu Shiba, a cute shiba dog wearing round glasses and a tweed jacket, stands next to a long, unrolling scroll character labeled 'WAL' (Write-Ahead Log) and a smiling tree character with a hard disk drive for a trunk, labeled 'DISK-TREE'. The WAL scroll has a distinct red stamp marker labeled 'CHECKPOINT'. The part of the scroll *before* the checkpoint is greyed out and faded. Professor Shiba points to the marker, saying in a speech bubble: 'Start from the last checkpoint!' Panel 2 (Top Right): A close-up on the WAL scroll *after* the 'CHECKPOINT' marker. Professor Shiba points excitedly at a series of distinct, readable notes drawn on the scroll (e.g., small drawings of a nut, a water drop, a leaf with checkmarks). Professor Shiba says: 'Read forward from here.' Panel 3 (Bottom Left): Professor Shiba follows the scroll to its very end, which is ragged, torn, and emitting cartoon smoke and squiggles. The notes on this torn section are illegible scribbles. Professor Shiba makes a dismissive gesture at the torn end, saying: 'Broken tail? Ignore it!' Panel 4 (Bottom Right): Professor Shiba holds a tidy stack of the valid notes collected from Panel 2, labeled 'list of LogOps' with a bow on it, handing them to the happy Disk-Tree character. The Disk-Tree holds a large rubber stamp ready to mark its trunk. Professor Shiba says: 'Apply these. Then make a new checkpoint!' The Disk-Tree gives a thumbs-up.

story_effectiveness.notes: The comic perfectly illustrates the Write-Ahead Log recovery process. Each panel clearly depicts a step: starting from the checkpoint, replaying valid logs, ignoring the corrupted tail, and applying the changes to the disk before creating a new checkpoint. The visual metaphors are intuitive and effective.

visual_consistency.notes: The hand-drawn, crayon style with wobbly outlines is flawlessly maintained across all four panels, matching the requested Julia Evans aesthetic. Characters (Professor Inu Shiba, Disk-Tree), objects (WAL scroll, checkpoint stamp), and overall layout are entirely consistent.

pleasing.notes: The charming, cute art style makes a complex technical topic approachable and engaging. The composition is clean, uncluttered, and aesthetically pleasing, with a great balance of visual elements and text.
